上个月的澳大利亚餐厅Crux发布的视频显示了一些影响他们周日早午餐的任何影响者所期望的东西：一把刀慢慢地在荷兰那岛拍了一个鸡蛋，因为任何好的鸡蛋是 - 直到金黄蛋糕渗出。但外观正在欺骗，并且由于覆盖到剪辑上的文本明确表示：是的，它是素食主义者。是的，它是流行的。
  对于素食主义者，乳酸素食者和其他任何削减卵消耗的人，长期以来几种方法可以取代鸡蛋的功能。亚麻籽，Chia种子，香蕉，苹果酱和淀粉混合物，如eNer-g蛋换剂，鸡蛋的绑定任务很好地进行了味道，可以在味道中找到。豆腐可以加扰，鹰嘴豆粉可以煮成薄，煎蛋型薄板。当素食主义看到一个令人兴奋的鼎盛时期时，其他商业蛋换剂选择正在上升。
  沿着您的心灵，这位基于工厂的空间的50岁的领导者，2015年推出了一种争吵的替代品。VEGG销售蛋蛋白替代品，包括粉末，意味着模拟法式吐司等菜肴中的蛋黄的味道和质地。 Juggernaut只是鸡蛋，其绿豆的液体争先恐后的鸡蛋，在2018年争抢美国。到今年3月，当刚收到卡塔尔投资机关的额外投资2亿美元，公司卖了相当于1亿鸡蛋。华盛顿邮政报道，2019年，2019年，美国人在每年吃大约279个鸡蛋 - 最高50年的鸡蛋消耗最高。
  但是，商业素食产品尚未捕获的特殊快乐：鸡蛋蛋黄在其所有粘性，球状，食物色情 - 荣耀。毕竟，尤利，与“把鸡蛋放在上面”的放纵Instagram吸引力有关，是否是“它”是披萨，汉堡还是早餐哈希。对于那些不想完全放弃鸡蛋概念的人，素食主义者鸡蛋用柔滑的白人和球形液体蛋黄是难以捉摸的但不是遥不可及的。这就是家庭和餐厅厨房的厨师有多欺骗科学，使基于工厂的Yolk POP成为可能。
  在2018年在阿德莱德开设的症状，提供了两种方式的素食蛋。鸡蛋本尼迪克特含有他们的偷猎，它为蛋黄的白人和西红柿使用腰基。早餐三明治采用了煎蛋的传真，配有闪亮，大豆的白人，含有花边边缘和蛋黄的相同泡沫。虽然你可以告诉蛋黄的主要组成部分是西红柿和大豆，但是，两个版本都是在Crux Owner George Thomson说的那种版本中的白人的质地和味道。 “客户不断评论我们所做的事情被认为是”巫术“。
  开发Crux的素食蛋“绝对消耗”汤姆森，培养皿大约五年来。 “我看到人们在网上制作素食主义者'煎蛋'，我想，我可以做得更好，”他说。 YouTube单独提供许多方法。 2018年，Ellie Blowen的博客elsa的健康生活共享了一个非常模仿的视频，其中她将南瓜融入黄色混合物中，然后将其Dolpoped它送到由面粉和椰奶制成的圆圈上。 Emmymade的emmy cho在使用鸡油果上的食谱上悄悄地，Canistel的口语名字;当它成熟时，与中美洲最原产树的树的水果相比，与煮沸的蛋黄的质地相比。然而，这些食谱尚未缩短，是“蛋黄”并没有吞噬，而是一勺酱油在有效的白色煎饼。
   未加工的鸡蛋蛋黄是蛋白质，脂肪，水和矿物质的液体混合物，其包裹在透明膜中，使蛋黄与构成蛋清的蛋白分开。为了使素食蛋蛋黄表现得类似，然后复制膜至关重要。 
在20世纪40年代获得专利的食品科学家William Peschardt，球形化是一种捕获薄凝胶膜中液体的技术。 Peschardt建议它用于“人工食用樱桃，软片等”。该过程通过混合含有藻酸钠的可食用液体，然后将其滴入盐浴中。 （还有逆向球化，涉及将含钙物质滴入藻酸盐浴中。）当藻酸钠达到钙离子时，离子粘合在一起，液体浸出凝胶皮。
  Flacherification在2003年真正进入公众意识，当时西班牙餐厅El Bulli  - 赢得了上一年的最佳餐厅，每年从2006年到2009年开始使用它并获得橄榄果汁制成的“橄榄”这样的菜肴赞誉。 El Bulli和Chef FerranAdrià是烹饪运动中的领导者，有时被称为“分子美食”，尽管许多与之相关的厨师使用“现代化的烹饪”。
  归功于物理学家Nicholas Kurti和ChemistHervé，其约会于1988年，分子美食是一个研究领域，探讨了家庭和餐厅厨房的烹饪背后的科学。 （“食品科学”与比较，一般是指工业粮食生产，安全和营养的研究。）该术语也被误认为是百科全书的不列颠诺坦尼卡 - 指的是使用科学创造新的菜肴和烹饪技巧。由于1990年代末，1990年代末，“分子美食”等厨师的工作，即“分子美食，”尽管潜在的错误数，但却成为泡沫，凝胶和球体等格式的食物的代名词。
  曾以鸡蛋的热爱闻名的Dufresne甚至是椰子 - 胡萝卜混合物，旨在看起来像他纽约市餐厅WD-50的阳光壮丽的鸡蛋，从2003年到2014年开放。（2007年，Dufresne的“鸡蛋”成为了在线厨师的Marcel Vigneron“撕开了”撕开了“网络蛋”时的“撕裂了”的争议的主题。）
  今天，Crux-of Comper-Sustes Facherized将番茄蛋蛋黄转化为番茄蛋黄。使用西红柿的类似技术在2019年的Insider视频中普及关于洛杉矶素食餐厅CrossRoads厨房，它自从2016年以来一直为番茄蛋黄提供服务。新的Paltz，纽约的Lagusta甜美的委员会于2016年开业，创造了它的自己的“分子蛋”在拉面顶部提供。但是，当Buzzfeed在诸如此写作中有近400万视图的视频中尝试时，Crossroads厨房的番茄技术在去年的善意时甚至更加关注。该方法正在Tiktok上的回合：除了Crux的剪辑之外，番茄素素食鸡蛋出现在User @ Oprahwinfrey.car1本月早些时候的Tiktok中，目前拥有350万次景观。 Crux还使用与腰果混合物的球形化，以使其其余的素食荷包蛋。
  Lisa Kitahara Food Blog Okodici厨房开发了她的素食蛋蛋黄食谱，因为她错过了Tamago Kake Gohan，生蛋的舒适食物混合在炎热的米饭中，当她的家人每年访问日本时，她会吃。她还使用了球形化技术，而不是西红柿，她发现“太多汁”，她发现了使用南瓜，胡萝卜或甜土豆的成功，使蛋黄基础是光滑和黄油的蛋黄。
  Kitahara在Tamago Kake Gohan的另一个关键成分是Nagaimo，这是一个山地山药，在加入蛋黄之前，她将腌制到她的碗里。 “这有点模仿鸡蛋的粘性纹理，”她说。她还在素食主义者中使用Nagaimo，以Kaki Fry或日式炸牡蛎。在那篇论述中，长头队以鸡蛋的方式束缚她用来挖掘牡蛎蘑菇的混合物，也增加了一种粘着的雪，这使得来自海洋的牡蛎。 
蛋黄比表格更多，许多素食蛋食谱中的常见风味元素包括kitahara和汤姆森是kala namak，也被称为印度人或喜马拉雅黑盐。与含有活性炭的夏威夷黑盐不同，Kala Namak含有硫磺化合物，使鸡蛋的气味和味道不同。其中一个是硫化氢，这是鸡蛋的味道，从饥饿诱导腐烂的污垢。
  为什么这些技术在商业甚至餐厅的意义上都不会更受欢迎可能是因为努力成为制造它们。汤森开发了一个成功的素食蛋原型之后，他的下一个挑战是在更大的规模上进行的，这是一个花了几个月的过程。 “制作少量素食主义者的鸡蛋是一件事，这是一个在商业环境中创造成千上万的不同游戏，”他说。“ “素食主义者'煎蛋'和素食主义者'偷猎鸡蛋'即使在完善两种方法之后，也会采取巨大的能量。”
  虽然Kitahara在她的博客上为她的素食蛋蛋黄进行了食谱，但她计划继续尝试这些过程。她喜欢最终解决一个版本的onsen tamago（“温泉蛋”中的日语），鸡蛋在低于沸腾的温度下慢慢煮熟，直到它们柔滑，柔软，番荔枝。她认为这可以通过使用琼脂来实现这一常用素食替代。 “即使是现在，我又非常兴奋地对食谱有点兴奋，”Kitahara说。也许有一天，她建议，她可以为每个人的偏好制作完美的素食蛋的指导;她想弄清楚蛋黄混合物中的根蔬菜的正确比例，以创造从流y蛋糕到更厚的更熟练的看似的液体的选择。
  在Crux，素食主义者蛋黄如此准确，这两种菜肴目前都有一个免责声明，“请小心鸡蛋。” “在菜单上的警告之前，人们对人们低估了他们的常见情况，他们最终会在素食主义者蛋黄中覆盖，”汤姆森说。
note_en:
A  video posted by the Australian restaurant  Crux on TikTok last month shows something you’d expect from any influencer filming their Sunday brunch: A knife slowly breaks an egg—blanketed in hollandaise, as any good eggs Benedict is—until golden yolk oozes out. But appearances are deceiving, and as the text overlaid onto the clip makes clear: Yes, it’s vegan. And yes, it pops.
  For vegans,  lacto-vegetarians, and anyone else cutting their egg consumption, there have long been many ways to replace the functions of the egg. Flax seeds, chia seeds, bananas, applesauce, and starch blends like Ener-G Egg Replacer perform the binding tasks that eggs do so well—a guide to which can be found  in Taste. Tofu can be scrambled, and chickpea flour can be cooked into a thin, omelette-esque sheet. Other commercial egg replacer options are on the rise as veganism  sees an exciting heyday.
  Follow Your Heart, the 50-year-old leader in the plant-based space, launched a substitute intended for scrambles  in 2015.  Vegg sells egg replacer products including a  powder meant to simulate the taste and texture of egg yolks in dishes like French toast. The juggernaut is  JUST Egg, whose mung bean-based liquid that scrambles like real eggs, hit the United States  in 2018. By March of this year, when JUST  received a $200 million investment backed by the Qatar Investment Authority,  the company had sold the equivalent of 100 million chicken eggs. Chicken eggs remain big business:  In 2019, Americans were eating about 279 eggs per person per year—the highest egg consumption in almost 50 years, the  Washington Post reported.
  But there’s a particular pleasure that commercial vegan products haven’t yet captured: the egg yolk in all of its gooey, globular, food porn-y glory. The yolk, after all, has much to do with the indulgent Instagram appeal of “ putting  an egg  on it,” whether “it” is a pizza, a burger, or a breakfast hash. For those who don’t want to give up the concept of the egg entirely, the vegan egg with silky whites and a spherical liquid yolk is elusive but not out of reach. This is how obsessed cooks in home and restaurant kitchens have tinkered with science to make the plant-based yolk pop possible.
  Crux, which opened in Adelaide in 2018, offers vegan eggs two ways. The eggs Benedict contains their poached version, which uses a cashew base for the whites and tomatoes for the yolks. The breakfast sandwich features their facsimile of a fried egg, complete with shiny, soy-based whites with lacy edges and the same bubble of a yolk. Though you can tell that the egg yolks’ main components are tomatoes and soy, the texture and flavor of the whites in both versions is spot on, says Crux owner George Thomson. “Customers are constantly commenting about how what we do is considered ‘wizardry.’”
  The process of developing Crux’s vegan eggs “absolutely consumed” Thomson, who has been vegan for about five years. “I saw people making vegan ‘fried eggs’ online, and I thought,  I could do that better,” he said. YouTube alone offers many methods. In 2018, Ellie Bullen of the blog Elsa’s Wholesome Life shared a much-imitated video in which she  blended pumpkin into a yellow mixture and then dolloped it onto a circle made of flour and coconut milk. Emmy Cho of Emmymade  riffed on that recipe using eggfruit, the colloquial name for canistel; when it’s ripe, the fruit, which comes from a tree native to Central America,  is compared to the texture of a hard-boiled egg yolk. Where these recipes fall short, though, is that the “yolks” aren’t poppable, but rather a spoonful of sauce atop what is effectively a white pancake.
   A raw chicken egg yolk is a liquid mixture of protein, fat, water, and minerals that’s encased in a  transparent membrane, which keeps the yolk separate from the albumen that makes up the egg whites. To make a vegan egg yolk that behaves similarly, then, replicating that membrane is crucial.
  Patented in the 1940s by food scientist William Peschardt, spherification is a technique that traps a liquid in a thin gel membrane. Peschardt suggested it for use in “artificial edible cherries, soft sheets, and the like.” The process works  by mixing an edible liquid that contains no calcium with sodium alginate, and then dropping it into a calcium salt bath. (There’s also  reverse spherification, which involves dripping a calcium-containing substance into an alginate bath.) When the sodium alginate meets the calcium ions,  the ions bind together, giving the drops of liquid a gel skin.
  Spherification really entered public consciousness in 2003, when the Spanish restaurant El Bulli—which won World’s Best Restaurant the previous year, and every year from 2006 to 2009— began using it and gained acclaim for dishes like  an “olive” made out of olive juice. El Bulli and chef Ferran Adrià were leaders in the cooking movement that’s sometimes referred to as “molecular gastronomy,” though many chefs associated with it use terms like “ modernist cuisine.”
  Credited to physicist Nicholas Kurti and chemist Hervé This and dating to 1988,  molecular gastronomy is a field of study that explores the science behind cooking in home and restaurant kitchens. (“ Food science,” by comparison, generally refers to the study of industrial food production, safety, and nutrition.) The term was also used—mistakenly,  according to Encyclopedia Britannica—to refer to the use of science to create new dishes and cooking techniques. Thanks to the work of chefs like Adrià, Heston Blumenthal, and Wylie Dusfresne in the late 1990s to early 2000s, “molecular gastronomy,” despite the potential misnomer, became synonymous with foods in formats like foams, gels, and spheres.
  Dufresne, who was known for his love of eggs, even served a  coconut-carrot mixture designed to look like a sunny-side-up egg at his New York City restaurant WD-50, which was  open from 2003 to 2014. (In 2007, Dufresne’s “egg” became the subject of brief food world controversy when  Top Chef’s Marcel Vigneron “ ripped [it] off” with his “ cyber egg” that was featured in  Wired.)
  Today, Crux—which is focused on “boundary-pushing vegan food”—uses spherification to turn a tomato-based mixture into its vegan egg yolk. A similar technique using tomatoes was popularized online in a  2019 Insider video about the Los Angeles vegan restaurant  Crossroads Kitchen, which has been serving a tomato yolk  since at least 2016. New Paltz, New York’s  Lagusta’s Luscious Commissary, which opened in 2016, created its own “ molecular egg” to serve on top of ramen. But Crossroads Kitchen’s tomato technique got even more attention last year when Buzzfeed’s  Goodful tried it in a video that has almost 4 million views as of this writing. The method is now making the rounds on TikTok: In addition to Crux’s clips, a tomato-based vegan egg appears in  a TikTok posted by the user @oprahwinfrey.car1 earlier this month and which currently has 3.5 million views. Crux also uses spherification with a cashew-based mixture to make the rest of its vegan poached egg.
  Lisa Kitahara of the food blog  Okonomi Kitchen developed her  vegan egg yolk recipe because she missed the tamago kake gohan, a comfort food of raw egg mixed into hot rice, that she would eat when her family visited Japan every year. She also uses the spherification technique, but instead of tomatoes, which she found “too juicy,” she’s found success using squash, carrots, or sweet potatoes to make an egg yolk base that’s smooth and buttery.
  Another key ingredient in Kitahara’s take on tamago kake gohan is nagaimo, a mountain yam, which she grates raw into her bowl of rice before adding the egg yolk. “It kind of mimics that slimy texture that eggs have,” she said. She also uses nagaimo in her  vegan take on kaki fry, or Japanese-style fried oysters. In that recipe, nagaimo acts in the place of eggs to bind the mixture she uses to dredge oyster mushrooms, and it also adds a sliminess that’s reminiscent of an oyster from the sea.
  There’s more to an egg yolk than form, and a common flavor element in many vegan egg recipes including Kitahara’s and Thomson’s is  kala namak, also known as Indian or Himalayan black salt. Unlike  Hawaiian black salts that contain activated charcoal, kala namak  contains sulfur compounds that evoke the smell and flavor of eggs. One of these is hydrogen sulfide,  which is responsible for the smell of eggs, from hunger-inducing to spoiled rotten.
  Why these techniques aren’t more popular in a commercial or even restaurant sense might be because of the effort that goes into making them. After Thomson developed a successful prototype of his vegan egg, his next challenge was making it on a larger scale, a process that took months. “It’s one thing to make a handful of vegan ‘poached eggs,’ it’s a whole different game to create thousands of them in a commercial environment,” he said. “Both the vegan ‘fried eggs’ and vegan ‘poached eggs’ take tremendous amounts of energy to create, even after perfecting both methods.”
  Though Kitahara has put a recipe for her vegan egg yolk on her blog, she plans to continue experimenting with the process. She’d like to eventually tackle a version of  onsen tamago (“hot springs eggs” in Japanese),  eggs slowly cooked at a temperature below boiling until they’re silky, soft, and custardy. She thinks this could be possible with the use of agar, a common vegetarian alternative to gelatin. “Even now, I'm still very excited to kind of tweak the recipe a bit,” Kitahara said. Perhaps one day, she suggested, she could make a guide to the perfect vegan egg for each person’s preference; she’d like to figure out the right proportions of liquid to root vegetables in her yolk mixture to create options from runny yolks to thicker, more cooked-seeming ones.
  At Crux, the vegan egg yolks are so accurate that both dishes currently come with a disclaimer to “please pop eggs with care.” “Before the warning on the menu, it was a common occurrence for people to underestimate just how realistic they were, and they would end up covered in vegan egg yolk,” Thomson said.
title: 挤压，可口素食蛋蛋黄的科学
title_en: The Science of the Squeezable, Poppable Vegan Egg Yolk

杰夫贝罗斯有一个隐藏的武器：你的数据。虽然亚马逊的零售帝国建立在一个复杂的基础设施和Myky工作实践中，但其销售成功是基于对数百万人们每天购买和浏览的复杂知识。
 亚马逊已经痴迷于您的数据，因为它是在线书店。近二十年前，该公司的首席技术官Werner Vogels表示，该公司试图“收集尽可能多的信息”所以它可以为人们提供建议。并且，随着亚马逊的扩展，它具有其数据收集操作。 “他们碰巧出售产品，但他们是一个数据公司，”前亚马逊主管在2020年告诉BBC。
 亚马逊对你很了解很多。您在亚马逊的生态系统中所做的一切：从您的应用程序或网站上的数千个搜索到您所做的每一个单击，滚动和鼠标移动。这是大量数据 - 这只是它的开头。从亚马逊那里要求他们的数据的人已经发送了数百个文件，包括他们的购物历史十年和亚历克萨设备录制的数千个语音剪辑。
  “通过亚马逊在线购物的原因是如此方便的是，公司在公民自由集团隐私国际公民利用集团公司的企业数据剥削计划总监Sara Nelson说，该公司已花费巩固其权力和触及。 “该公司能够通过其购物平台来收集大量数据，也可以通过其环形摄像机，Alexa语音助手，网络服务，送货服务，流媒体服务及其许多其他业务流”。现在亚马逊正在进入医疗保健 - 纳尔逊所说的事情。
 据报道，亚马逊的数据收集也将其放在监管机构的错误方面。 6月10日，华尔街日报报告卢森堡的数据保护稳压器，亚马逊的欧洲总部是基础，正准备4.25亿美元的GDPR罚款，以回应它使用人民个人数据的方式 - 尽管没有提供具体细节，但亚马逊发言人拒绝评论潜在的罚款。反竞争监管机构也在看公司的数据使用。政府要求亚马逊的更多数据，包括来自戒指和alexa录音的信息。
   让我们从亚马逊的隐私声明开始。在超过4,400字的单词中，大多数人都没有读过它，这一点令人惊讶，但它确实清楚地铺设了亚马逊对你的数据做了什么。广泛地说，亚马逊收集您的信息来自三个来源。这些是：当您使用Amazon（及其其他服务（如阅读Kindle Books）时，您可以自动收集的数据（关于您的手机和您所在位置的信息），最后，从第三次获取信息缔约方（信贷检查，以了解您的帐户是否欺诈，例如欺诈。
  所有这些数据收集的最终目标？帮助销售你更多的东西。亚马逊将使用您的个人信息 - 以及它可以了解您的喜欢和不喜欢的一切 - 向您展示它认为您可能购买的东西的建议。更广泛地还可以了解最受欢迎的卖家和人们的购物行为。 
“关于购物的个人数据非常敏感，”牛津大学伦理学院副教授CarissaVéliz说。 “它可以告诉你一个人的健康状况，政治倾向，他们的性行为等等。人们在亚马逊上购买各种各样的东西，从书籍和电影到与健康有关的物品。从Alexa添加到那个个人数据，它更加有关。“
  它还使用信息等信息，以确保您购买的东西实际上已交付给您。 “我们处理您的个人信息以运营，提供和改进我们为客户提供的亚马逊服务，”该公司的隐私声明说。它还广泛地列出了它收集的所有数据的法律论据。
 让我们来看看你给亚马逊的信息。您应该假设您在亚马逊网站，应用程序或其任何产品上所做的一切都以某种方式保存。您在亚马逊上放置的每个订单，每次显示在素质上观看，您在亚马逊音乐中聆听的每首歌都会跟踪和存储Alexa的每个请求。
 亚马逊的隐私政策表示您可能 - 根据您的设置以及您决定交出的设置，给予它：您的名字;地址和电话号码;银行明细;年龄;地点;您的联系人中的人员，电子邮件和地址;您上传到您的个人资料的任何照片;播放列表;观看列表;愿望人;录音; Wi-Fi凭据;信用历史信息，如果您在亚马逊，增值税和其他业务信息上销售物品。
 亚马逊的自动信息收集的东西是事情更令人毛骨悚然的地方。这是关于您如何以及使用Amazon产品的数据。自由撰稿人Riccardo Coluccini被送出一张桌子，其中12,048行详细说明了他在亚马逊的网站上制作的所有点击。 “值涉及访问特定页面时的日期和时间，使用的IP地址和设备，地理位置 - 如果可能的话 - 基于IP地址，以及提供互联网服务的电信公司的名称，”他在2018年写道。同样，对亚马逊的其他数据请求显示Kindle Logs日期，花时间阅读，以及复制或突出​​显示书籍的频率。同样，戒指铃铃记录他们检测到的每次运动记录，并且在环形应用程序中进行每个水龙头。
 亚马逊的隐私声明它可能会自动收集IP地址;登录详细信息;计算机的位置;使用其服务时，您的设备日志错误;您的应用偏好;饼干细节;标识符链接到手机或计算机;以及您单击的所有URL，包括页面交互信息“如滚动，点击和鼠标播放”。公司收集和记录您的产品所携带的所有互动并不罕见 - 它们可用于使它们更好或更好地识别错误 - 但这些信息很快就会增加。亚马逊表示，它收集的数据可用于改善其服务以及遵守法律义务和其他目的。 “我们不是在销售客户的业务中＆＃39;其别人的个人信息，“它的隐私声明说。
 亚马逊的最终信息类型是关于您的是来自第三方。如果送货公司发现您提供的一个问题，这可以包括更新的送货地址;从“我们运营共同标志企业的商家”的帐户和购买信息;有关亚马逊子公司的“互动”的信息（有很多人，他们拥有自己的隐私政策）;有关您与Alexa相关联的设备的信息;和信用历史，它可以作为其努力检测欺诈的一部分。 
停止亚马逊完全跟踪您是不可能的 - 如果您要使用亚马逊购物，那么亚马逊将收集您的数据 - 但您可以采取几个步骤来限制它可以收集和使用的信息。其中一些由亚马逊本身提供，而其他人则涉及调整浏览器设置并使用其他工具。
  首先，如果您对数据的兴趣亚马逊已经存储了关于您的信息，那么您可以使用其下载工具来访问其中的一些。这将仅为您提供亚马逊的子集，并且您必须使主题访问请求获取所有内容。
  除此之外，亚马逊有一些控件可以提高您的隐私。例如，Alexa和Ring有自己的隐私集线器，您可以在那里删除录制和管理隐私设置。但对于大多数亚马逊信息，您将需要您的主帐户。您可以根据您的兴趣和喜欢的信息，关闭亚马逊显示您的个性化广告 - 虽然您仍然会根据您在亚马逊上的之前的购买（它们可以调整，但未关闭，但在这里）仍然会看到建议。您还应该考虑关闭广告cookie，允许第三方收集您的信息。亚马逊的第三方Cookie Partners列表包括超过75家公司，从Facebook到手机游戏巨型国王。
 您还可以在Amazon上关闭您的浏览历史记录 - 尽管这是值得争取的。 “亚马逊可以保持浏览历史隐藏，”读取一个Amazon设置页面。 “当您关闭浏览历史时，我们不会显示您单击的项目，或从此设备中搜索。”这并不意味着数据被删除。 véliz说它可以非常难以让亚马逊删除个人数据。 “你会发现没有办法删除你的购买历史 - 这一切都没有，甚至不是你在十年前买的东西，”她说。 “你能做的最好的事情是隐藏你自己的购买历史，这是荒谬的，甚至不得有时不起作用。” （归档订单可以从您的ORDERS页面完成，其中包括您拥有Amazon帐户的每年的下拉菜单）。
 超出亚马逊自己的工具，如果您想限制亚马逊可以跟踪多少，那么您需要使用隐私浏览器。这些可以自动禁用第三方cookie并停止在Web上跟踪。如果您不希望搜索链接到您的Amazon帐户，那么您也需要确保您的退出。还可以选择 - 和购买 - 直接从制造商或其他在线卖家购买产品。而且，如果您真的不想被亚马逊跟踪，那么有核选项：删除您的Amazon帐户。
note_en:
Jeff Bezos has a hidden weapon: your data. While Amazon’s retail empire is built on a complex web of infrastructure and  murky working practices, its selling success is based on an intricate knowledge of what millions of people buy and browse every day.
 Amazon has been obsessed with your data since it was an online bookshop. Almost two decades ago the firm’s chief technology officer, Werner Vogels,  said that the company tries to “collect as much information as possible” so it can provide people with recommendations. And, as Amazon has expanded, so has its data collection operation. “They happen to sell products, but they are a data company,” a former Amazon executive told  the BBC in 2020.
 Amazon knows a lot about you. Everything you do in Amazon’s ecosystem: from the thousands of searches you make on its app or website to every individual click, scroll and mouse movement you make. It’s a lot of data – and that’s just the beginning of it. People who have requested their data from Amazon have been sent  hundreds of files, including a decade of their  shopping history and thousands of voice clips recorded by Alexa devices.
  “The reason online shopping through Amazon is so convenient is because the company has spent years consolidating its power and reach,” says Sara Nelson, director of the corporate data exploitation programme at civil liberties group Privacy International. “The company is in a position to collect huge amounts of data – through its shopping platform, but also through its Ring cameras, Alexa voice assistants, web services, delivery services, streaming services, and its many other business streams”. And now Amazon is  moving into healthcare – something that Nelson says is concerning.
 Amazon’s data collection is also reportedly putting it on the wrong side of regulators. On June 10, the  Wall Street Journal reported data protection regulators in Luxembourg, where Amazon’s European headquarters is based, are preparing a  $425 million GDPR fine in response to the way it uses people’s personal data – although no specific details were provided and an Amazon spokesperson declined to comment on the potential fine.  Anti-competition regulators are also looking at the company’s use of data. And governments are  demanding more data from Amazon, including information from Ring and Alexa recordings.
   Let’s start with Amazon’s  privacy notice. At more than 4,400 words it’s hardly surprising that most people don't read it, but it does clearly lay out what Amazon does with your data. Broadly, the information that Amazon collects about you comes from three sources. These are: the data you give it when you use Amazon (and its other services, such as reading Kindle books), the data it can collect automatically (information about your phone and your location) and, finally, information it gets from third-parties (credit checks to find out if your account is fraudulent, for example).
  The ultimate goal of all this data collection? To help sell you more things. Amazon will use your personal information – and everything it can learn about your likes and dislikes – to show you recommendations for stuff it thinks you might buy. More broadly it can also get a sense of its most popular sellers and people’s shopping behaviour.
 “Personal data about shopping is incredibly sensitive,” says Carissa Véliz, an associate professor at the University of Oxford’s Institute for Ethics. “It can tell you about a person’s health status, their political tendencies, their sexual practices, and much more. People buy all kinds of things on Amazon, from books and movies to health-related items. Add to that personal data from Alexa, and it gets even more concerning.”
  It also uses information, such as your location, to make sure the things you buy actually get delivered to you. “We process your personal information to operate, provide, and improve the Amazon Services that we offer our customers,” the company’s privacy notice says. It also broadly sets out the legal arguments for all the data it collects.
 Let’s look at the information you give to Amazon. You should assume that everything you do on Amazon’s website, apps or any of its products is saved in some way. Every order you place on Amazon, every show you watch on Prime, every song you listen to on Amazon Music and every request you make of Alexa is tracked and stored.
 Amazon’s privacy policy says you might – depending on your settings and how much you’ve decided hand over – give it: your name; address and phone number; bank details; age; location; the names, emails and addresses of people in your contacts; any photographs you’ve uploaded to your profile; playlists; watchlists; wishlists; voice recordings; Wi-Fi credentials; credit history information and, if you sell items on Amazon, VAT and other business information.
 The automatic information Amazon collects is where things get a bit more creepy. This is all data about how and when you use Amazon products. Freelance journalist Riccardo Coluccini was sent a table with 12,048 rows detailing all the clicks he made on Amazon’s website. “The values concern the day and time when a specific page is visited, the IP address and the device used, the geolocation – if possible – based on the IP address, and the name of the telecommunication company that offers the internet service,” he  wrote in 2018. Similarly, other data requests to Amazon show how  Kindle logs the date, time spent reading, and how often you copy or highlight parts of books. Likewise,  Ring doorbells log every record of motion they detect and each tap made within the Ring app.
 Amazon’s privacy notice details that it may automatically collect your IP address; login details; the location of computer; errors your device logs when using its services; your app preferences; cookie details; identifiers linked to your phone or computer; and all the URLs that you click, including page interaction information “such as scrolling, clicks, and mouse-overs”. It’s not uncommon for companies to collect and record all the interactions you make with their products – they can be used to make them better or identify bugs – but this information quickly adds up. Amazon says the data it collects can be used to improve its services as well as complying with legal obligations and other purposes. “We are not in the business of selling our customers' personal information to others,” its privacy notice says.
 The final type of information Amazon collects about you is that from third-parties. This can include updated delivery addresses if a delivery company finds there’s a problem with the one you provided; account and purchase information from “merchants with which we operate co-branded businesses”; information about “interactions” with Amazon’s subsidiaries (there’s a  lot of them and they have their own privacy policies); information about devices you’ve linked with Alexa; and credit history it gets as part of its efforts to detect fraud.
  It’s impossible to stop Amazon tracking you completely – if you’re going to shop with Amazon then Amazon will collect your data – but there are a few steps you can take to limit the information that it can gather and use. Some of these are provided by Amazon itself, while others involve tweaking your browser settings and using other tools.
  First, if you’re interested in the data Amazon has stored about you, then you can use its download tools to  access some of it. This will only provide you with a subset of what Amazon has and you’ll have to make a  subject access request to get everything.
  Beyond this, there are some controls that Amazon has that can improve your privacy.  Alexa and  Ring, for instance, have their own privacy hubs where you can delete recordings and manage privacy settings. But for the majority of Amazon information, you’ll need your main account. You can  turn off Amazon showing your personalised ads based on what it infers about your interests and likes – although you will still see recommendations based on your previous purchases on Amazon (they can be tweaked, but not turned off,  here). You should also consider  turning off advertising cookies, which allow third-parties to collect your information. Amazon’s list of  third-party cookie partners includes more than 75 companies, ranging from Facebook to mobile gaming giant King.
 You can also turn off your browsing history on Amazon – though the impact of this is debatable. “Amazon can keep your browsing history hidden,”  reads one Amazon settings page. “When you turn your browsing history off, we will not show items you click on, or searches that you make from this device.” That doesn’t mean the data is deleted. Véliz says it can be incredibly difficult to get Amazon to delete personal data. “You’ll see that there is no way to delete your purchasing history – none of it, not even the things you bought a decade ago,” she says. “The best thing you can do is hide your purchasing history from yourself, which is ridiculous, and even that doesn’t work well sometimes.” (Archiving orders can be done from  your orders page, which includes a drop-down menu for each year you’ve had an Amazon account).
 Beyond Amazon’s own tools, if you want to limit how much Amazon can track you then you’ll need to use a  privacy-browser. These can automatically disable third-party cookies and stop you from being tracked across the web. If you don’t want searches to be linked to your Amazon account then you need to ensure you are logged out, as well. There’s also the option of researching – and buying – products directly from manufacturers or other online sellers. And, if you really don’t want to be tracked by Amazon at all, there’s the nuclear option:  delete your Amazon account.
title: 亚马逊的所有方式跟踪您以及如何停止它
title_en: All the ways Amazon tracks you and how to stop it
